title: Working with the part that believes happiness is for other people
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
I found myself saying it again last week 

 I was sitting on my couch, scrolling through photos of a friend's birthday party. Everyone was laughing, arms...
htmlContent:
I found myself saying it again last week

I was sitting on my couch, scrolling through photos of a friend's birthday party. Everyone was laughing, arms around each other, faces lit up in that way that only happens when people feel safe and seen. And I felt it. That familiar tightening in my chest. That quiet voice that said, "That's for them. Not for you."

I've been doing this work for years now. I know about parts. I know about protectors and exiles and all the ways our nervous system keeps us small. But knowing doesn't always stop the feeling. And that's okay. Here's the thing: that part of me that believes happiness is for other people? It's not wrong. It's not broken. It's just doing a job it learned to do a long time ago.

You probably know this part too. Maybe it shows up when you see a couple holding hands and you feel that ache. Maybe it appears when someone offers you genuine kindness and you instinctively pull back. Maybe it whispers, "Don't get too comfortable. This won't last."

I want to talk about this part directly. Not as something to get rid of or fix, but as something to understand. Because the parts that believe we don't deserve happiness are often the ones that saved us when happiness was actually dangerous.

Where this part comes from

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), talks about how our parts develop in response to our environment. When you grow up in a home where safety is unpredictable, where love comes with strings attached, where your needs are consistently unmet, your system learns something very specific: hope hurts.

Think about it. When you're a kid and you let yourself feel excited about something, and then it gets taken away, or you get punished for wanting it, or someone shames you for being "too much" - your brain starts making connections. Happiness equals disappointment. Joy equals pain. Wanting equals rejection.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, would say this is a protective response stored in the body. Your nervous system learns to brace before the blow. It learns to scan for threat even in moments of peace. The part that believes happiness is for other people is doing exactly what it was designed to do: keeping you safe from the crash that always follows the high.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying this to make you feel broken. I'm saying it because when you understand the logic of your system, you can stop fighting yourself. That part isn't your enemy. It's your most loyal protector.

I remember a client who told me she couldn't accept a promotion at work because she "knew" she'd mess it up eventually. She could feel the shame coming before she even took the job. Her part was trying to save her from the humiliation of failing later. It made perfect sense. It was also keeping her stuck in a life that felt too small.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic helped me understand that this part isn't just a thought pattern. It's a survival strategy that's been running for years, sometimes decades.

How this part shows up in daily life

You might notice this part in small moments. Someone compliments your work and you deflect. A friend invites you to something fun and you make an excuse. You achieve something you worked hard for and instead of celebrating, you immediately focus on what's next.

I do this all the time. I'll finish a big project and within hours I'm already worrying about the next one. My system doesn't know how to rest in accomplishment. It only knows how to prepare for the next threat. Right?

This part also shows up in how we relate to other people's happiness. Have you ever felt a little stab of resentment when someone else gets what you want? That's not jealousy, necessarily. That's your part saying, "See? They get it. You don't. This proves everything I've been telling you."

And here's where it gets tricky. Because this part can be really convincing. It has evidence. It has years of proof. You can point to specific moments in your life where hoping led to hurt. Your part isn't making this up. It's just applying old data to new situations, situations where the rules might have changed.

Janina Fisher, who writes about trauma-informed stabilization treatment, talks about how these parts are stuck in time. They're still operating from the age when the wound happened. So when you're 35 and you feel that familiar dread about being rejected, it might actually be your 8-year-old self who learned that wanting love was dangerous.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helped me see that even the parts I thought were "bad" or "wrong" were just trying to help in the only way they knew how.

What happens when you try to bypass this part

The wellness industry loves to tell you that you just need to "choose happiness" or " abundance" or "." And if you're like me, hearing that stuff makes something inside you want to scream. Because it's not that simple. It's not that you don't want to be happy. It's that your system has a very good reason for not trusting happiness.

When you try to bypass this part with positive affirmations or forced gratitude, you're telling your protector, "You're wrong. You're bad. Go away." And what does any protector do when it feels attacked? It doubles down. It gets louder. It finds more evidence.

I tried the bypass approach for years. I read the books. I did the journaling. I told myself I was worthy of love and abundance and all of it. And underneath, that part was still there, whispering, "You don't really believe that, do you?"

Wild, right? The more I tried to force happiness, the more disconnected I felt from it.

What actually works is slower. It's less glamorous. It involves sitting with the part that believes happiness is for other people and saying, "I see you. I hear you. Thank you for trying to protect me."

This doesn't mean you agree with the part. It doesn't mean you let it run your life. It means you stop fighting it. You create enough space to have a different relationship with it.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. Her work on RAIN (Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture) gave me a way to be with my parts without trying to change them instantly.

Small steps toward a different relationship

So what do you actually do with this part? Here's what I've found helpful. Not as a cure, but as a practice.

First, notice when the part shows up without judgment. Just name it. "Oh, there's that part that says happiness isn't for me." That's it. You don't have to argue with it or fix it. Just acknowledge it.

Second, get curious about what the part is afraid would happen if you let yourself be happy. Really listen. Maybe it's afraid you'd get too comfortable and let your guard down. Maybe it's afraid you'd lose your edge or your motivation. Maybe it's afraid you'd be alone in your joy because no one else in your family ever seemed to have any.

I asked my part this question once, and it told me something that broke my heart. It said, "If you let yourself be happy, you'll forget how to survive. You'll stop being vigilant. And then something terrible will happen and you won't see it coming." That part was trying to keep me alive. It wasn't trying to ruin my life.

Third, experiment with small doses of happiness. Not the big, scary kind. Not the "I deserve everything I've ever wanted" kind. Just tiny moments. A good cup of coffee. A warm blanket. A few minutes of sitting in the sun. Let yourself feel it for five seconds. Then let it go. You can build tolerance slowly.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something tighten? Did you feel a little resistant? That's okay. That's just your part doing its job.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because sometimes the part that believes happiness isn't for you needs help learning how to let good things in and keep harmful things out.

What recovery looks like here

I wish I could tell you that this part eventually goes away. It doesn't. At least not for me. What changes is my relationship with it. I can hear that voice saying "happiness is for other people" and I can respond with something like, "I know you're scared. I'm going to try this anyway. And I'll be here no matter what happens."

Recovery isn't about silencing the part. It's about becoming big enough to hold both things: the fear and the hope. The part that believes happiness isn't for you, and the part that's willing to try anyway.

Some days I can do this. Some days I can't. And that's part of the practice too.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," talks about how trauma lives in the body and how healing requires experiences that contradict what our system has learned. So maybe healing this part isn't about convincing it with words. Maybe it's about giving it small, safe experiences of happiness that slowly teach it something new.

A warm hug that doesn't get pulled away. A compliment you let yourself receive without deflecting. A moment of peace where nothing bad happens. These are the experiences that can slowly shift the part's belief system. Not overnight. Not through force. Through repetition and gentleness.

Sit with that. Your part learned to believe happiness wasn't for you because it had evidence. It can learn to believe something different if you give it new evidence. Small evidence. Consistent evidence. Evidence that doesn't lie.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
